title: Helianthus buphthalmoides
intro: Helianthus buphthalmoides, also known as the false sunflower, is a perennial plant species native to North America, characterized by its daisy-like flowers and edible seeds. The plant has been traditionally used for its medicinal properties, which include anti-inflammatory and antiseptic effects, making it beneficial for treating wounds, skin conditions, and respiratory issues. The bioactive constituents of Helianthus buphthalmoides include flavonoids, phenolic acids, and terpenoids, which are responsible for its medicinal properties. In herbal medicine, Helianthus buphthalmoides is often used to make teas, infusions, and tinctures, as well as being used in traditional remedies for treating a variety of ailments.
taxonomy: Helianthus buphthalmoides is classified within the family Asteraceae, which is a large and diverse group of flowering plants. This species is further classified under the genus Helianthus, commonly known as sunflowers. Within the genus, Helianthus buphthalmoides is placed in the section Helianthastrum. The classification of Helianthus buphthalmoides is based on its morphological characteristics and genetic relationships with other sunflower species. Helianthus buphthalmoides is native to the southwestern United States and northern Mexico, and its classification is closely related to its geographical distribution.

constituents: Helianthus buphthalmoides, also known as the desert sunflower, contains a variety of active constituents, including sesquiterpene lactones, which have been shown to possess anti-inflammatory and antioxidant properties. The plant also contains flavonoids, such as apigenin and kaempferol, which have been found to have antimicrobial and antiproliferative effects. Additionally, Helianthus buphthalmoides contains polyphenolic compounds, including quercetin and myricetin, which have been reported to exhibit antioxidant and anti-inflammatory activities. The plant's sesquiterpene lactones may also contribute to its potential anti-cancer properties. Further research is needed to fully understand the active constituents and potential therapeutic applications of Helianthus buphthalmoides.
